item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone from a scientific background who also has a deep and abiding dislike of complex mathematics (particularly physics) I found this scip barely comprehensible. The discussion helped fill in some of the larger gaps for me but overall I have to agree with other folks who suggested footnotes for the mathematical luddites out there (like me). I'll just go back to dissections and gut content analyses where the worst maths I have to deal with involves plugging values into set formulae. -1 for now but would love to +1 after some footnotes were added.

item.title: 200th upvote!
item.content:encoded: Proud to be the 200th upvote on this great and spooky page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No voting here.
I love the general idea of the time distorsion and the difficulty of having to deal with one. I also like the anomalous guy trying to escape the anomaly without realising his own time is horribly out of sync with the rest of the universe.
But the way it's presented is way too obscure for my tastes, to the point that I'm not sure I would have understood what was going on without reading the comments or knowing beforehand what this was about.
Possibly it would simply need a few more paragraphs to reduce the density of the infodump, or, as suggested by many other posters, some footnotes to explains the concepts behind (and what a w-axis is supposed to be).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This definitely needs footnotes to explain the spatiotemporal anomaly further to be less impenetrable, but based on my rereads and the posts here, I appreciate this SCP pretty well, especially the danger of relatively experienced time. What I worry though is what would happen to any of the Agents if they die while being in the anomaly? Would they have the same negative explosive effect as the blue guy if they stop moving at one point to rearrange the statues?

item.title: Random Article Review #13, 7 December 2016
item.content:encoded:
I'm settling on a neutral vote here.

Points in favor: This is a good example of conveying a narrative without the use of an unnecessarily long format, something we seem to have forgotten since the heyday of Series II and something I'm very much interested in seeing return. The idea of the Foundation badly misunderstanding what they have on their hands and desperately trying to clean up is also done well here, and that's rare; most "Foundation dun goofed" stories involve some exceedingly boneheaded or obvious mistake that robs the premise of any semblance of believability. I think that problem is mostly avoided here.

Points against: Under different circumstances, this would feel very much like the author is trying to baffle the readership with bullshit by employing technical lingo from a field that I'm certain maybe 1% of us actually understand (I certainly don't). The author's discussion posts alleviate that concern for me, though. Mostly, I feel like a narrative opportunity was missed in making the catalyst for this phenomenon a random set of four statues. It could be replaced with almost any other random object and the story wouldn't change. For a piece that is (rightly) asking the reader to piece things together on their own, I feel like a deepening of the mystery could have done very efficiently by giving the catalyst some sort of further angle to think about. Not a convoluted, extended backstory, but a couple more breadcrumbs could make this into something really memorable. Instead it feels incomplete as a narrative to me.

I wouldn't have a problem referring other authors to this piece as an example of a competently-done, entertaining article (especially done in a style that is rarer nowadays). But I also am held back from actively supporting it due to what I consider a pretty significant imperfection in the construction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems like the Blue Man can control his movements just fine. But The friction with the air when he escapes will be the problem, not the full conversion of his kinetic energy into heat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
resulting in massive thermal damage to Site 53 and surrounding populated areas.

Is this a deliberate understatement? I calculate 7×1017 J. Which is equivalent to 170 Mt TNT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Articles like this need to stop existing, its nearly impossible to understand anything here without being a goddamn scientific genius.

Plenty of people liked it enough to upvote, so this article will continue to exist.

Calm down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Articles like this need to stop existing, its nearly impossible to understand anything here without being a goddamn scientific genius. Downvoted for having too much jargon when none is needed,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really cool and I love it. One suggestion…

On high-speed video, gait is consistent with a brisk walk.

"High-speed video" usually refers to video taken with a high-speed camera (one with a very high shutter speed, allowing for an enormous number of frames recorded per second), and therefore produces recordings of events that can be played back at a fraction of the speed at which they happened. (Example)

I think what you're going for here is "time-lapse video" or "time-lapse photography", the technique where a long period of time is recorded and then played back faster than life, resulting in a short recording. (Example)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. This is why the Foundation didn't know about him when they first found the anomaly. Thanks to the time dilation, the team they first sent in to contain the anomaly still doesn't know about him, which is why they've sent in new people to tell them (before he escapes)…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm, I interpreted that as the stuff falling over in the 4th dimension (time); so basically, if you bring a slanted thing too close to another it will make them tip over, and everything that isn't too slanted will just be nudged a bit but not enough to tip over on it's own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The blue man is moving at 2/5 the speed of light, so unless I'm seriously misunderstanding the nature of light speed, the information would reach our eyes only 2.5 times faster than the speed at which blue man is moving (which would still be incredibly slow).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: perhaps, but even the level of slowing/speeding described would be negligible when compared to the speed of light. That is why the blue man represents a distinct effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Wait, if time is slowed down, the movement of electromagnetic waves should be slow too, so shouldn't we experience a severe discrepancy between where everything is actually placed in the corridor and where we see it being placed ("lag," so to speak)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the most confusing thing I've ever read on this site and there is heavy competition for that spot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I love Ophite's SCPs, and this is no exception. I could figure out pretty much what was going on from the article, but the comments helped (Like the Blue Man being the artist). Of course from his perspective, he's just walking across the hall away from these statues that just toppled over. He's got no idea whats happened or how long he's been in there or what all these strange light blurs are everywhere…

Best thing to do would probably be to clear a path/build a tunnel away from him, through the horizon, into space. That way he gets shot into space and takes most of the energy with him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What are the containment agents inside seeing from the blue man?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's possible. But the containment team hasn't decided what they want to do yet — one of the possible routes to containment is to actually make the singularity worse. Delaying the exit by a factor of 100 would contain it for the next 40,000 years. And the people at the front of the conga-line are the ones in the best position to do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Doesn't help in the slightest. Actually, it makes things much worse.

The blueshifted man, who's going at relativistic speeds, won't decelerate if he's dead.

Not decelerating=exiting the area at relativistic speeds= mass destruction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I'm a scientist, I consider the expendature of a couple dozens lives to be less important than the alternative. You're not destroying the scenario, just the players involved, and protecting the greater good. I'd imagine an explosion involving a force moving at .41c would have planet-altering consequences. This would simply be a demotion to D-class for anyone trapped inside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Or is that just too easy.

More like it's too "Destroy, Destroy, Destroy", which doesn't quite equate "Secure, Contain, Protect".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Send someone in with a machine gun, waste everybody.

Even at relativistic speeds, bullet > man in terms of speed.

Close off the area. SCP neutralized.

Or is that just too easy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's the problem of the air. We… don't know why he isn't just encapsulated in a giant fireball right now. Even if we move the wall, he's going to become a massive plasma fireball sometime on the other side, and accelerate into space. And let's just hope this is nowhere near a mountain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As much as I love this SCP, couldn't it be downgraded to Safe by simply removing the wall that the Blue Man would run into once the statues are moved? I.e., the man running into a wall and causing a gigaton explosion stops posing a problem if there is no wall for him to run into. As far as I understand, the wall he would run into is outside of the affected radius, and could be torn down hundreds of years before the Blue Man would reach it.

Then again, even without the wall, he would sooner or later (relatively speaking) hit something that would cause this explosion.

Alternately, when abruptly leaving the anomalous zone via the first team succeeding, would even his first footstep hitting the ground carry enough kinetic energy to cause some massive damage, destroying the Blue Man in the process. This having the unfortunate side-effect, of course, of creating a 70kg grenade of human shrapnel moving at relativistic speeds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit.

Really loved this. Although…couldn't the Foundation just send someone with a tranquilizer gun to intercept 1272-6?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, it's basically Relativistic Baseball but with a hell of a lot more mass, only ~45% the speed of the baseball, and Weird Spacetime Shit causing it to gradually ramp up from "why is he blue" to "why are we a crater the size of Texas".

On an unrelated note, if the w-axis is a fourth spatial dimension, how do they know the statues are "tilted", let alone precisely how much?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's intentionally unclear, but the blue man is actually the artist who designed the original structures. He's been trapped on the other side of a topological anomaly for the past sixty years, is now topologically anomalous himself, and is trying to get out of the Escher hell he's been trapped in for a long, long time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Overall, it's good. I just don't get where the blue man came from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd love to see some subtle change to the last paragraph to reflect this information.

Because holy shit.

On the whole, the article was pretty impenetrable for me. But the last part I got enough to understand that blue guy was no bueno. But there's nothing for the layman like myself to understand exactly the Michael Bay level of destruction we're dealing with here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The problem I have with the blue man is that he feels tacked on. If it were something like a spacial ripple, or a block of unknown matter, I'd get it, it would fit (the generation of the distortion caused something to move at massive speed, but due to the distortion it won't be exploding any time soon). But the man seems out of place; he must have massive anomalous properties himself, in addition to any properties of the area, to be able to stroll while moving 0.4C

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, someone actually did the math re: how dangerous the blue man is. Here's a note I received on the subject.

"I can't quite seem to sign up to the SCP wiki, but I wanted to share my thoughts on your SCP. I was going to post this in discussions (if you wish to share, feel free):

So to recap, and to make sure I have this correct:

1. The statues cause a very minor distortion in spacetime in their general area / are each slightly "ajar" in the spacetime axis compared to the rest of our reality. (The "w" axis.)
2. These statues were brought too close to another spacetime distortion, and the combined effect caused a local spacetime anomaly. (Alternately: the 4 statues combined caused a very severe distortion?)
3. This spacetime anomaly causes time to pass logaritmically slower the closer to the center you get. Since it is a singularity, this means at the exact center time becomes infinitely slow (in reality, much like a black hole — a gravitational singuarity — a person could travel forever and not reach the center). In reality, much like a Black Hole, it would be impossible to ever reach the direct center — time would slow to nothing before you reached it.
4. Several teams were sent into the anomaly to move the statues and break up the anaomly.
5. After these teams were sent in, someone discovered someone who was very near the center of the anomaly when it started was walking out of it when the anomly occured. This is the "blue man."
6. More worrying, the light coming off the man is heavily, heavily blue shifted, meaning that relative to the outside world, he is moving outrageously fast.
7. Specifically, he is moving at .41c relative to the rest of the universe. This suggests if the anomaly is abruptly stoppped without someone catching him and getting him to slow down first, he will continue moving at .41c — or around 315,189,815.63 miles per hour.
8. At relativistic speeds such as this, he would not merely make a loud thud when he hits the wall — in fact, Einstein's theory of relativity lets us figure out exactly what would happen. To wit:

Specifically, if we take his mass as being roughly 47 - 141 (skinny to fat) with 70kg being the "average" mass of an adult male, and combine this with Einstin's relativistic formula for kinetic energy:

K = mc^2 ( L - 1 )

L is the Lorentz Factor, or the inverse square root of 1 minus the velocity squared divided by the speed of light squared: 1/(sqrt(1-v^2 / c^2)) = around 1.096388 in this case.

(The Lorentz factor is why moving at the speed of light is mathmatically impossible, becuase V=C would resolve to 1/0, V>C would resolve to imaginary numbers)

Mass = 70kg, as mentioned above.

c = 299792458 m/s by physical law

So the energy released would be:

70 * 299792458 * 299792458 * (1.096388 - 1) = 6.06407*10^17 joules of energy, or roughly 1.5 gigatons of TNT, plus or minus a gigaton depending on how heavy the man is.

For reference, a typical modern nuclear bomb is only .3 kiloton to 1.2 megaton, meaning this would be roughly equivalent to a thousand nuclear bombs going off all at once.

Give or take a few hundred.

If the Foundation cannot stop themselves from fixing this anomaly before catching the blue man, or can't stop the blue man from exiting the anomaly first, then the resulting explosion will be… impressive. From a geological standpoint, that is.

References:
Einstein's Relativistic Formula for Kinetic Energy: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Relativistic_kill_vehicle#Calculating_energy_content
Average mass of an adult male: http://hypertextbook.com/facts/2003/AlexSchlessingerman.shtml
Joules fo Megaton Conversion: http://www.unitconversion.org/energy/joules-to-megatons-conversion.html"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps the effect is stronger in between the status, and the blue guy is closer to that centre point - so close that even .41c is frozen. With the 1973 team only needing to reach the nearest statue and move it out, they won't have to go as 'deep' into it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my first SCP and still needs some clarity edits, which I've been delaying because I'm working on something else — my thought was that the anomaly is not entirely uniform on approach, and that teams walking at one side of the hallway may go substantially more quickly than those elsewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well yeah, the blue guy is kind of what I'm referring to. Right now the containment procedure is 'stop the pre-2003 team and have them leave it as it is so we have a chance to come up with something about the blue guy', but that's not permanent and blue-guy is going to pop out of there soon.

Hell, at .41c, surely there's no way that the pre-2003 team will come close to reaching the statues before he exists the phenomenon anyway, so I'm not sure why they're even bothering to try and stop them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We've sent people in to fix it. Fixing it is relatively simple. The problem is that we can't send people in to fix it at a reasonable pace — and that unless we can deal with the blue guy, 'fixing' it may cause him to shoot out of the anomaly at .42c.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, okay, I think I see your angle. I've got a pretty solid conceptual understanding of SR/GR, but the problem here is one of wording: 'SCP-1272-06 is presently exiting the vault at 41% of the speed of light' doesn't specify a reference frame. If he's moving very rapidly in the anomaly reference frame, we factor in the sluggishness of anomaly time vs. external time, and outside observers then conclude: 'he's coming out of there like a bullet, and if the time dilation stops, he'll [airburst, I guess]'. I read it this way - he's moving at .41 C in the anomaly frame. But I think maybe I goofed, and it's supposed to read as something else?

I've confused myself just thinking about it. Whatever the intended meaning, though, I think he's the least interesting part of the whole SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I'm not an astronomer, but I understand it thusly:

The man doesn't need to be an SCP. Time is relativistically slower as you approach the epicenter of the anomaly. So to the observers, everyone in there is really freaking slow. The man coming out from the center is moving from slower to normal, so he appears to be speeding up. This means he can blueshift. He doesn't need to be special, to the observers, he is speeding up; to him, he is walking OUT of whatever the breach made. I assume as the agents get closer to the anomaly, they will appear red. But then, as I understand, to blue shift is not really to turn blue, just to decrease wavelength which -leads- to blue… but Engineering Physics was like 7 years ago for me.

I love this SCP. It started off not scary at all, but then as I read it and deciphered the science it become more and more horrifying. I am wowed at a time anomaly done in an awesome way, then I consider "Who the hell is walking OUT?" Great stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. Interesting. I love the setup, and I can follow all the topology without a problem. I read the first half with rising interest, but the introduction of the blueshifted relativistic man really killed the buzz - it's a dull, cartoonish concept in an SCP that trades mostly on quasi-hard SF concepts and tensions. The elements of a good SCP should lead the mind to nervous speculation, but our semiluminal friend just gets me thinking about all the ways that he could ruin what was, up to that point, a perfectly interesting SCP for the mathematically inclined.

If you need something to erupt at relativistic speeds make it mysterious; a man moving at a brisk trot doesn't do anything to stoke the imagination beyond 'ugh, I bet he's a smug humanoid SCP' or 'the air resistance must be something fierce'. Otherwise, though, I really like where this is headed. Downvote for now, but very prepared to flip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"At pre-screening, containment agents should be advised that at present topological density, successful containment is expected to be completed on March 18, 2394, 423 years from initial entry."

What is this referring to? It seems to imply that the Foundation expects to be able to shut this down and free them at that date, but the rest of the article implies that they currently have no known solution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed. Good eye.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"uncrecognizable"

Your spelling is uncrecognizable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *sigh* I think the opposite, but then, the article seems to work regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah. I… hm. Okay. I knew I was wrong, but also hoped no one would do the math — I need people to be able to get in within a period of hundreds of years, but I also need the blue dude to be exiting frighteningly quickly. Now that I see that including technical details will induce people to actually check whether they're correct, I'll go back and do another cycle of revision.

On a totally different topic, do you mind if I use the prehistoric quasi-Hebrew speaking culture from the weather drones again? In particular, do you have a definite, absolutely-necessary definition for that repeated nationalist phrase?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another math nitpick…

SCP-1272-6 is said to have travelled 4 meters since it was first spotted 9 years ago. Based on the 184 nanoseconds = 1 hour figure, 16 seconds have passed within the anomaly since then. Do the math on 4 meters in 16 seconds and you get a speed of 0.9 km/h, which is definitely far short of 0.41C.

Either the creature should be moving much faster, the time compression should be much more severe, or I really don't understand relativity. I wish i'd read that book by that wheelchair guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I think I've narrowed down that that's the problem. I just need a tonally correct way to convey the same information — and at the moment, I can only vacillate between 'not technical enough' and 'way too technical.' In essence, here's what happened.

Imagine a number of pennies on a string, all laying flat. That string is the 'w' axis, and everything in the universe has the same orientation toward the string. All the pennies are at a 'w' axis deviation of zero. Now we rotate one of the pennies so that it's at an angle relative to all the other pennies. The angle of that penny, relative to all the other pennies, is its w-axis deviation, in degrees. Bring too many twisted pennies together, and it begins to deform the shape of the string itself. Bring them way too close, and the string itself starts to knot.

What we're dealing with here is the knot in the string. We brought too many 'twisted pennies' together, and it done fucked up spacetime but good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Though unknown at the time, permitting objects with summed w-deviations of greater than 38 degrees to approach within 41 meters of a topological anomaly results in a topological involution which rapidly evolves toward a nongravitational singularity.

I completely understand this, who said I don't??

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has to be linked to the relativistic treadmill in some way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah. We've essentially got a conga line of agents going in. Though we basically just put one containment team in in 1974, then have been sending a crapton of agents in (two every four months) since 2003 to try and stop them, and report new containment protocols to those already inside the anomaly. I may have to change that, however. That seems like an unreasonable waste.

It's not as bad as that, however, because time speeds up logarithmically on approach to the singularity. Agents close to the vault are going incredibly slowly, but agents who have just entered take a few steps, slow down considerably, then essentially slow to a complete stop as they reach the vault doors. If you walk in, change your mind, and walk back out, you could conceivably be in and out of there in a few years. Subjectively, you just spent a couple seconds in there, panicked, and then ran back out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Makes sense, I suppose.

Another issue. We're sending someone into the anomaly ever four months. If 184 nanoseconds pass within the anomaly for every hour outside, my math says that's only .6 of a second that passes within, so essentially we've got like a conga line of agents heading for the door. Moreover, it's been only slightly more than one minute that's passed since the anomaly formed in '73.

Or maybe I just don't understand relativity. Upvoting anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The other article written by me. It's fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderfully written physics. I like how the Foundation has to keep sending people to stop the previously sent people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because it's moving at 0.41 lightspeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really good. I love the inclusion of SCP-1272-6 and the whole "Remember, the Foundation really doesn't know what it's doing, ever" vibe.

My one… well, I can't really call it a suggestion, because I don't know how to fix it, but you basically restate the containment procedures in the description, specifically the parts about the various distances, and I found that very tedious to read a second time. …I guess you could, say, designate the distance between 47 and whatever km as some kind of Zone (Zone Omega, Bunyip Horizon, whatever, give it a name) and then at least you wouldn't have to respecify the details.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed this in some other article. "Per" and "via" are part of the English lexicon now, there's no need to italicize them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It took me a couple reads to figure this out, and i'm still not entirely sure I understand it.

Why will SCP-1272-6 exiting the time warp result in destruction?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, except that usually when the term "singularity" is used to describe a phenomenon, it's generally assumed to refer to a gravitational singularity, such as one finds in a black hole. Technically, it refers to any mathematical or topological singularity, but that's not the way it's most often used.

The article is a bit thick; I didn't fully understand it until I got to the comments, because I'm only casually familiar with topography, but what I made out was quite intriguing. Could use some footnotes to explain things like what a w-axis is, and how many spatial dimensions are required to describe the phenomenon…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I
may just love topology a whole fucking lot

but this SCP does it perfectly and so I love this SCP a whole fucking lot

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What? I might just be ignorant, but this is impenetrable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
w-axis deviations

You might want to have a footnote on what the "w-axis" is, even if the footnote is blacked-out or redacted.

nongravitational singularity

If by that you mean "a singularity which isn't the center of a black hole", you can just say "singularity", since the context isn't going to make anyone think you're talking about a black hole. Or you could call it a "time singularity" or "topological singularity"
